21

27	
28	[visual: covary] The probe sweeps through directions; each score bar climbs and dies as the probe turns toward and away from its candidate; after the sweep the probe parks and the four scores land as integers on the bars.
29	
30	Now turn the probe and watch the bars answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the same, right? Watch again. The bars moved while the candidates never moved at all. The score reads agreement of direction, not sameness.
31	
32	## beat-03: Name the machine you just used
33	
34	[visual: connect] The three probe positions replay as stills: along, across, against, each with its frozen score. The stills collapse into one lilac meter dial whose needle swings from oppose through ignore to agree as the sweep replays.
35	
36	Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply and add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.

39

file.content:
# Script: dotprod

Final video narration, one section per storyboard beat. Narration
paragraphs are the spoken words, read at roughly 150 words per minute, with
no em-dashes and no emojis. Bracketed `[visual:]` paragraphs are the
video's stage directions: what is on screen while the words are spoken.

## beat-01: The score behind the song

[visual: connect-to-reality] Two columns of numbers slide in side by side, one labeled with the listener's taste and one with the song; paired entries light up in periwinkle and a single lilac score readout counts up as the comparison lands.

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

[visual: connect-to-reality] The question card holds over the frozen score, half the frame deliberately empty.

So here is the question of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers? Hold that question. One answer to it runs half of modern machine learning.

## beat-02: Turn the probe and feel it

[visual: dynamic-concept] One amber probe arrow and four periwinkle candidate arrows appear; under each candidate a lilac score bar with a numeric readout sits at its current value.

Strip it down. A taste is an arrow: each direction is a quality, and the length is how hard the song leans that way. Take one probe arrow and four candidate arrows. Multiply them entry by entry and add. That single sum is the score.

[visual: covary] A freeze frame carries the prediction card before anything moves.

Before you drag anything, call it: which candidate ends up on top, and does any score go negative?

[visual: covary] The probe sweeps through directions; each score bar climbs and dies as the probe turns toward and away from its candidate; after the sweep the probe parks and the four scores land as integers on the bars.

Now turn the probe and watch the bars answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the same, right? Watch again. The bars moved while the candidates never moved at all. The score reads agreement of direction, not sameness.

## beat-03: Name the machine you just used

[visual: connect] The three probe positions replay as stills: along, across, against, each with its frozen score. The stills collapse into one lilac meter dial whose needle swings from oppose through ignore to agree as the sweep replays.

Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply and add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.

[visual: symbol-sense] The aha sentence trues up on screen, freehand to exact with a mint glow. A small periwinkle mono annotation, a . b, sits on the score readout.

So say it plainly: the dot product is a similarity meter. Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. And the reading tracks direction, because direction is all the sweep ever changed.

## beat-04: What the meter gets wrong

[visual: dynamic-process] The retrieval prompt holds as a card while the meter idles.

Time to close the loop. Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

[visual: dynamic-process] One periwinkle candidate stretches longer; its lilac score climbs while its direction holds still; a small rose flag marks the unearned win.

While you think, one more honest look. Stretch a candidate longer and its score climbs, even though its direction never moved. Long songs would win on sheer loudness.

[visual: connect-to-reality] The walk-home question lands as the closing card, and a quiet chip points to the extensions menu on the page.

For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size? Still up? The menu below has three ways to keep going.

# Storyboard: dotprod (S3, schema v2). The per-beat contract for the M6
# round-trip testbed session, "the dot product as similarity". Authored and
# locked 2026-07-04 as the M6 seed (the production graph consumes this; the
# attention session's S3 machinery produced its equivalent at M5).
# Narration here matches script.md exactly; the script adds the [visual:]
# cue timeline per beat. Timing budgeted at ~150 spoken wpm.
schemaVersion: 2
session: dotprod
aha: "The dot product is a similarity meter."
openingQuestion: "How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?"
closingRetrievalPrompt: >-
  Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations
  turn them into the score?
breadcrumbQuestion: >-
  What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?
extensionsMenuRef: extensions.yaml
notationSchedule:
  - symbol: a . b
    referent: the multiply-and-add score
    firstManipulatedBeat: beat-02
    firstSymbolicBeat: beat-03
beats:
  - id: beat-01
    title: The score behind the song
    narration: >-
      Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have
      never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got
      compared, and the comparison said: same taste. So here is the question
      of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?
      Hold that question. One answer to it runs half of modern machine
      learning.
    visual: >-
      [visual: connect-to-reality] Two columns of numbers slide in side by
      side, one for the listener's taste and one for the song; paired
      entries light up in periwinkle and a single lilac score readout counts
      up as the comparison lands. The question card holds over the frozen
      score, half the frame deliberately empty.
    tracks: [video, page]
    newIdea: >-
      Two lists of numbers can be compared into one score, and that one
      comparison runs recommendation.
    novelElements:
      - the two number lists
      - the match score readout
    onScreenText:
      - "How do you score a match?"
    colors:
      lists: data.observed
      score: data.params
      background: bg
      labels: text
    estimatedSeconds: 26
  - id: beat-02
    title: Turn the probe and feel it
    narration: >-
      Strip it down. A taste is an arrow: each direction is a quality, and
      the length is how hard the song leans that way. Take one probe arrow
      and four candidate arrows. Multiply them entry by entry and add. That
      single sum is the score. Before you drag anything, call it: which
      candidate ends up on top, and does any score go negative? Now turn the
      probe and watch the bars answer. Point at a candidate and its score
      climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the
      same, right? Watch again. The bars moved while the candidates never
      moved at all. The score reads agreement of direction, not sameness.
    visual: >-
      [visual: covary | dynamic-concept] One amber probe arrow sweeps
      through directions; four periwinkle candidate arrows hold still; under
      each candidate a lilac score bar with a numeric readout climbs and
      dies as the probe turns toward and away from it. A freeze frame before
      the sweep carries the prediction card; after the sweep the probe parks
      and the four scores land as integers on the bars.
    tracks: [video, page]
    newIdea: >-
      Multiply pairwise and add: the score moves continuously with the
      probe's direction, and agreement is not identity.
    novelElements:
      - the probe arrow
      - the candidate arrows
      - the score bars
    onScreenText:
      - "probe"
      - "candidates"
      - "3  2  0  -2"
    colors:
      probe: data.heat
      candidates: data.observed
      scores: data.params
      background: bg
      labels: text
    interactiveSpec: page/interactives/dot-alignment.yaml
    predictionPrompt:
      prompt: >-
        Before you drag anything, call it: which candidate ends up on top,
        and does any score go negative?
      expectedWrongAnswer: >-
        The candidate that looks most similar to the probe wins, and
        nothing goes negative because similarity cannot be less than zero.
    misconceptionBeat: true
    misconception: similarity-means-identical
    estimatedSeconds: 42
  - id: beat-03
    title: Name the machine you just used
    narration: >-
      Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a
      candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to
      zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply and
      add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the
      dial. So say it plainly: the dot product is a similarity meter. Two
      lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. And
      the reading tracks direction, because direction is all the sweep ever
      changed.
    visual: >-
      [visual: connect | symbol-sense] The three probe positions replay as
      stills: along, across, against, each with its frozen score. The stills
      collapse into one lilac meter dial whose needle swings from oppose
      through ignore to agree as the probe sweep replays. The aha sentence
      trues up on screen, freehand to exact with a mint glow. A small
      periwinkle mono annotation, a . b, sits on the score readout.
    tracks: [video, page]
    newIdea: >-
      The multiply-and-add score behaves like a meter for alignment: agree
      positive, across zero, oppose negative.
    novelElements:
      - the meter dial
      - the annotation a . b
    onScreenText:
      - "a . b"
      - "agree  ignore  oppose"
      - "The dot product is a similarity meter."
    colors:
      probe: data.heat
      candidates: data.observed
      meter: data.params
      ahaTrueUp: data.fit
      background: bg
      labels: text
    majorReveal: true
    estimatedSeconds: 36
  - id: beat-04
    title: What the meter gets wrong
    narration: >-
      Time to close the loop. Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go
      in, so what two operations turn them into the score? While you think,
      one more honest look. Stretch a candidate longer and its score climbs,
      even though its direction never moved. Long songs would win on sheer
      loudness. For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the
      direction and forgets the size? Still up? The menu below has three
      ways to keep going.
    visual: >-
      [visual: dynamic-process | connect-to-reality] The retrieval prompt
      holds as a card while the meter idles. Then one periwinkle candidate
      stretches longer; its lilac score climbs while its direction holds
      still; a small rose flag marks the unearned win. The walk-home
      question lands as the closing card, and a quiet chip points to the
      extensions menu on the page.
    tracks: [video, page]
    newIdea: >-
      The raw score rewards length as well as direction, and that gap is
      the next session's opening.
    novelElements:
      - the stretched candidate
      - the unearned-win flag
    onScreenText:
      - "two arrows in, one number out"
      - "keep the direction, forget the size"
      - "Still up?"
    colors:
      candidates: data.observed
      score: data.params
      failureFlag: data.error
      background: bg
      labels: text
    estimatedSeconds: 34

content.content:
1	---
2	session: dotprod
3	aha: "The dot product is a similarity meter."
4	spineVersion: 1
5	locked: 2026-07-04
6	scope: >-
7	  Plain dot product as an alignment score, in two dimensions, four
8	  candidates. No cosine, no matrices, no attention; those are breadcrumbs.
9	compromises:
10	  - id: toy-numbers
11	    phrase: "the numbers in this session are chosen for readability"
12	  - id: two-dimensions
13	    phrase: "real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions"
14	---
15	
16	# Spine: dotprod
17	
18	The session's constitution. Every downstream artifact answers to this file.
19	
20	## 1. The aha (canonical sentence, string-matched everywhere)
21	
22	**"The dot product is a similarity meter."**
23	
24	Falsification test the session must pass on screen: the probe sweep shows
25	the score climbing with alignment, reading zero across, and going negative
26	against; the meter framing survives the misconception check (a big score is
27	agreement of direction, not identity of items).
28	
29	## 2. The locked beat list (stable ids; one new idea each)
30	
31	| id | Beat | One-line intent | Est. sec |
32	|----|------|-----------------|----------|
33	| beat-01 | Hook | The recommendation moment: two lists of numbers agreed; how do you score a match? | 24 |
34	| beat-02 | Felt elemental unit | Multiply pairwise and add; the learner sweeps the probe and feels the score move; misconception (similarity means identical) voiced and refuted | 34 |
35	| beat-03 | Aha | The behavior gets its name: agree positive, across zero, oppose negative; the sentence trues up in mint | 28 |
36	| beat-04 | Close | Retrieval prompt, the length problem as the walk-home gap, extensions menu | 26 |
37	
38	Total 112s estimated; hard cap 540s (L10).
39	
40	## 3. The metaphor registry (with honesty boundaries)
41	
42	Primary, load-bearing beats 2 through 4: **the meter**. A physical dial
43	whose needle answers a comparison: agree, ignore, oppose. Exact as far as
44	sign and monotone-in-alignment go. Declared strains, disclosed in-session:
45	
46	1. the numbers in this session are chosen for readability; real embedding
47	   coordinates are neither small nor round (beat-02, page callout).
48	2. real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions; two dimensions is the
49	   picture, not the territory. Nothing in the mechanism changes with
50	   dimension count (beat-03, page callout).
51	
52	## 4. Color assignments (semantic cast, locked)
53	
54	| Concept | Token |
55	|---|---|
56	| the probe (the thing asking) | data.heat (amber) |
57	| the candidates (the data interrogated) | data.observed (periwinkle) |
58	| the score bars / meter needle | data.params (lilac) |
59	| the aha true-up | data.fit (mint) |
60	| labels | text (cream) |
61	| background | bg (10pm Sky) |
62	
63	## 5. Extensions sketch (Still up?)
64	
65	- talk lane: have an LLM defend, then attack, the meter metaphor; a good
66	  conversation surfaces the length problem unprompted.
67	- read lane: the attention paper (arXiv 1706.03762) is where tonight's
68	  score reappears inside a softmax; Bahdanau (arXiv 1409.0473) is where
69	  learned alignment scores first shipped.
70	- build lane: a NumPy meter over toy "song" vectors with a ranking check.
71

The session's constitution. Every downstream artifact answers to this file.

## 1. The aha (canonical sentence, string-matched everywhere)

| beat-03 | Aha | The behavior gets its name: agree positive, across zero, oppose negative; the sentence trues up in mint | 28 |
| beat-04 | Close | Retrieval prompt, the length problem as the walk-home gap, extensions menu | 26 |

Total 112s estimated; hard cap 540s (L10).

## 3. The metaphor registry (with honesty boundaries)

Primary, load-bearing beats 2 through 4: **the meter**. A physical dial
whose needle answers a comparison: agree, ignore, oppose. Exact as far as
sign and monotone-in-alignment go. Declared strains, disclosed in-session:

1. the numbers in this session are chosen for readability; real embedding
   coordinates are neither small nor round (beat-02, page callout).
2. real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions; two dimensions is the
   picture, not the territory. Nothing in the mechanism changes with
   dimension count (beat-03, page callout).

## 4. Color assignments (semantic cast, locked)

| Concept | Token |
|---|---|
| the probe (the thing asking) | data.heat (amber) |
| the candidates (the data interrogated) | data.observed (periwinkle) |
| the score bars / meter needle | data.params (lilac) |
| the aha true-up | data.fit (mint) |
| labels | text (cream) |
| background | bg (10pm Sky) |

## 5. Extensions sketch (Still up?)

- talk lane: have an LLM defend, then attack, the meter metaphor; a good
  conversation surfaces the length problem unprompted.
- read lane: the attention paper (arXiv 1706.03762) is where tonight's
  score reappears inside a softmax; Bahdanau (arXiv 1409.0473) is where
  learned alignment scores first shipped.
- build lane: a NumPy meter over toy "song" vectors with a ranking check.

content.text: Now I'll write the lesson following the beat order.

input.content:
# How does a data center decide two songs share your taste?

## The score behind the song

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. No human picked it for you. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

That is the whole magic trick, and it is smaller than it looks. One list stood for you. One list stood for the song. Something turned the pair into a single number, and that number was high enough to hit play.

So here is the question of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?

Hold that question before we reach for any definition. One answer to it runs half of modern machine learning. We are going to build that answer with our hands, not memorize it.

## Turn the probe and feel it

Strip it down until it fits in your palm. A taste is an arrow. Each direction is a quality, some axis like "loud" or "mellow," and the length along that direction is how hard the song leans that way. Two dimensions, so we can actually see the thing.

Now picture one amber probe arrow, that is you, and four candidate arrows, four songs sitting still. Here is the rule for scoring a match: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. That single sum is the score. One multiply, one add.

> Honest edges: the numbers in this session are chosen for readability. Real embedding coordinates are neither small nor round; they are long ugly decimals a machine never has to look at. We use tidy integers so your eyes can do the arithmetic the GPU does silently.

Before you read on, call it. Which candidate ends up with the highest score, and does any score go negative? Say an answer out loud. A prediction you can be wrong about is worth ten definitions you nod along to.

The tempting guess is that the candidate that looks most like the probe wins, and that no score dips below zero, because how could similarity be less than nothing?

Now turn the probe and watch the scores answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the same, right?

Watch again, more carefully. The scores moved while the candidates never moved at all. Nothing about the songs changed. Only your direction changed. So the score cannot be reading sameness of items. It is reading agreement of direction.

And yes, scores go negative. Point against a candidate and the sum drops below zero, because some of those pairwise products come out negative and drag the sum down. Similarity, it turns out, has a basement.

The number is not measuring what two things are. It is measuring which way they point.

## Name the machine you just used

Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs to its biggest. Point it across, at a right angle, and the sum dies to exactly zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative.

Three positions, three readings: agree, ignore, oppose. One multiply and one add, and it behaves like a meter with a needle that swings across those three words on a dial.

So say it plainly:

**The dot product is a similarity meter.**

Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. That is the machine. It has a name and a symbol now: we write it $a \cdot b$, the dot sitting between the two arrows to mean multiply-pairwise-and-add.

> Honest edges: real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions. Two dimensions is the picture, not the territory. Nothing in the mechanism changes when you add more axes; you still multiply entry by entry and add, there are just more entries. The dial in your head stays exactly as true at three hundred dimensions as it is at two.

And notice why the reading tracks direction and not size or identity. Direction is the only thing the sweep ever changed. The meter answers the question you actually moved.

## What the meter gets wrong

Time to close the loop. Do this from memory, not from scrolling up.

Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

If "multiply pairwise, then add" came back to you without a peek, you own $a \cdot b$ now. It is yours.

While that settles, one more honest look, because a meter you trust blindly is a meter that will lie to you. Take one candidate and stretch it longer without turning it at all. Its direction holds dead still. Its score climbs anyway.

That is a problem. It means a long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement. The meter cannot tell "points the same way" apart from "just bigger." A shout beats a whisper even when the whisper agrees with you more.

> Honest edges: the raw dot product rewards length as well as direction. We are shipping that flaw on purpose. It is not a bug we hid; it is the exact gap the next session opens on.

For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

Still up? The menu below has three ways to keep going: argue the metaphor with an LLM until it cracks, chase this same score into the attention papers where it reappears, or build the meter yourself in a few lines of NumPy.

# Script: dotprod

Final video narration, one section per storyboard beat. Narration
paragraphs are the spoken words, read at roughly 150 words per minute, with
no em-dashes and no emojis. Bracketed `[visual:]` paragraphs are the
video's stage directions: what is on screen while the words are spoken.

## beat-01: The score behind the song

[visual: connect-to-reality] Two columns of numbers slide in side by side, one labeled with the listener's taste and one with the song; paired entries light up in periwinkle and a single lilac score readout counts up as the comparison lands.

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

[visual: connect-to-reality] The question card holds over the frozen score, half the frame deliberately empty.

So here is the question of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers? Hold that question. One answer to it runs half of modern machine learning.

## beat-02: Turn the probe and feel it

[visual: dynamic-concept] One amber probe arrow and four periwinkle candidate arrows appear; under each candidate a lilac score bar with a numeric readout sits at its current value.

Strip it down. A taste is an arrow: each direction is a quality, and the length is how hard the song leans that way. Take one probe arrow and four candidate arrows. Multiply them entry by entry and add. That single sum is the score.

[visual: covary] A freeze frame carries the prediction card before anything moves.

Before you drag anything, call it: which candidate ends up on top, and does any score go negative?

[visual: covary] The probe sweeps through directions; each score bar climbs and dies as the probe turns toward and away from its candidate; after the sweep the probe parks and the four scores land as integers on the bars.

Now turn the probe and watch the bars answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the same, right? Watch again. The bars moved while the candidates never moved at all. The score reads agreement of direction, not sameness.

## beat-03: Name the machine you just used

[visual: connect] The three probe positions replay as stills: along, across, against, each with its frozen score. The stills collapse into one lilac meter dial whose needle swings from oppose through ignore to agree as the sweep replays.

Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply and add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.

[visual: symbol-sense] The aha sentence trues up on screen, freehand to exact with a mint glow. A small periwinkle mono annotation, a . b, sits on the score readout.

So say it plainly: the dot product is a similarity meter. Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. And the reading tracks direction, because direction is all the sweep ever changed.

## beat-04: What the meter gets wrong

[visual: dynamic-process] The retrieval prompt holds as a card while the meter idles.

Time to close the loop. Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

[visual: connect-to-reality] The song columns swap for two new ones labeled "you" and "the role": Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Matched entries multiply and the products pool into the same lilac readout on the same dial.

While you think, one detour worth naming. Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Different aisle, same meter. The multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant, and that is exactly why it travels.

[visual: dynamic-process] One periwinkle candidate stretches longer; its lilac score climbs while its direction holds still; a small rose flag marks the unearned win.

Now the honest look. Stretch a candidate longer and its score climbs, even though its direction never moved. Long songs would win on sheer loudness, and padded resumes would win on sheer length.

[visual: connect-to-reality] The walk-home question lands as the closing card, and a quiet chip points to the extensions menu on the page.

For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size? Still up? The menu below has three ways to keep going.

# Storyboard: dotprod (S3, schema v2). The per-beat contract for the M6
# round-trip testbed session, "the dot product as similarity". Authored and
# locked 2026-07-04 as the M6 seed (the production graph consumes this; the
# attention session's S3 machinery produced its equivalent at M5).
# Narration here matches script.md exactly; the script adds the [visual:]
# cue timeline per beat. Timing budgeted at ~150 spoken wpm.
schemaVersion: 2
session: dotprod
aha: "The dot product is a similarity meter."
openingQuestion: "How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?"
closingRetrievalPrompt: >-
  Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations
  turn them into the score?
breadcrumbQuestion: >-
  What is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?
extensionsMenuRef: extensions.yaml
notationSchedule:
  - symbol: a . b
    referent: the multiply-and-add score
    firstManipulatedBeat: beat-02
    firstSymbolicBeat: beat-03
beats:
  - id: beat-01
    title: The score behind the song
    narration: >-
      Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have
      never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got
      compared, and the comparison said: same taste. So here is the question
      of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?
      Hold that question. One answer to it runs half of modern machine
      learning.
    visual: >-
      [visual: connect-to-reality] Two columns of numbers slide in side by
      side, one for the listener's taste and one for the song; paired
      entries light up in periwinkle and a single lilac score readout counts
      up as the comparison lands. The question card holds over the frozen
      score, half the frame deliberately empty.
    tracks: [video, page]
    newIdea: >-
      Two lists of numbers can be compared into one score, and that one
      comparison runs recommendation.
    novelElements:
      - the two number lists
      - the match score readout
    onScreenText:
      - "How do you score a match?"
    colors:
      lists: data.observed
      score: data.params
      background: bg
      labels: text
    estimatedSeconds: 26
  - id: beat-02
    title: Turn the probe and feel it
    narration: >-
      Strip it down. A taste is an arrow: each direction is a quality, and
      the length is how hard the song leans that way. Take one probe arrow
      and four candidate arrows. Multiply them entry by entry and add. That
      single sum is the score. Before you drag anything, call it: which
      candidate ends up on top, and does any score go negative? Now turn the
      probe and watch the bars answer. Point at a candidate and its score
      climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the
      same, right? Watch again. The bars moved while the candidates never
      moved at all. The score reads agreement of direction, not sameness.
    visual: >-
      [visual: covary | dynamic-concept] One amber probe arrow sweeps
      through directions; four periwinkle candidate arrows hold still; under
      each candidate a lilac score bar with a numeric readout climbs and
      dies as the probe turns toward and away from it. A freeze frame before
      the sweep carries the prediction card; after the sweep the probe parks
      and the four scores land as integers on the bars.
    tracks: [video, page]
    newIdea: >-
      Multiply pairwise and add: the score moves continuously with the
      probe's direction, and agreement is not identity.
    novelElements:
      - the probe arrow
      - the candidate arrows
      - the score bars
    onScreenText:
      - "probe"
      - "candidates"
      - "3  2  0  -2"
    colors:
      probe: data.heat
      candidates: data.observed
      scores: data.params
      background: bg
      labels: text
    interactiveSpec: page/interactives/dot-alignment.yaml
    predictionPrompt:
      prompt: >-
        Before you drag anything, call it: which candidate ends up on top,
        and does any score go negative?
      expectedWrongAnswer: >-
        The candidate that looks most similar to the probe wins, and
        nothing goes negative because similarity cannot be less than zero.
    misconceptionBeat: true
    misconception: similarity-means-identical
    estimatedSeconds: 42
  - id: beat-03
    title: Name the machine you just used
    narration: >-
      Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a
      candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to
      zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply and
      add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the
      dial. So say it plainly: the dot product is a similarity meter. Two
      lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. And
      the reading tracks direction, because direction is all the sweep ever
      changed.
    visual: >-
      [visual: connect | symbol-sense] The three probe positions replay as
      stills: along, across, against, each with its frozen score. The stills
      collapse into one lilac meter dial whose needle swings from oppose
      through ignore to agree as the probe sweep replays. The aha sentence
      trues up on screen, freehand to exact with a mint glow. A small
      periwinkle mono annotation, a . b, sits on the score readout.
    tracks: [video, page]
    newIdea: >-
      The multiply-and-add score behaves like a meter for alignment: agree
      positive, across zero, oppose negative.
    novelElements:
      - the meter dial
      - the annotation a . b
    onScreenText:
      - "a . b"
      - "agree  ignore  oppose"
      - "The dot product is a similarity meter."
    colors:
      probe: data.heat
      candidates: data.observed
      meter: data.params
      ahaTrueUp: data.fit
      background: bg
      labels: text
    majorReveal: true
    estimatedSeconds: 36
  - id: beat-04
    title: What the meter gets wrong
    narration: >-
      Time to close the loop. Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go
      in, so what two operations turn them into the score? While you think,
      one detour worth naming. Swap the songs for a job posting. Score
      yourself against what the role wants: multiply the matched entries,
      add them up, one number for the fit. Different aisle, same meter. The
      multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant, and that is
      exactly why it travels. Now the honest look. Stretch a candidate
      longer and its score climbs, even though its direction never moved.
      Long songs would win on sheer loudness, and padded resumes would win
      on sheer length. For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that
      keeps the direction and forgets the size? Still up? The menu below
      has three ways to keep going.
    visual: >-
      [visual: dynamic-process | connect-to-reality] The retrieval prompt
      holds as a card while the meter idles. The song columns swap for two
      labeled "you" and "the role": Python hours, SQL reps, nights free;
      matched entries multiply and pool into the same lilac readout on the
      same dial. Then one periwinkle candidate stretches longer; its lilac
      score climbs while its direction holds still; a small rose flag marks
      the unearned win. The walk-home question lands as the closing card,
      and a quiet chip points to the extensions menu on the page.
    tracks: [video, page]
    newIdea: >-
      The raw score rewards length as well as direction, and that gap is
      the next session's opening.
    novelElements:
      - the job-fit columns
      - the stretched candidate
      - the unearned-win flag
    onScreenText:
      - "two arrows in, one number out"
      - "different aisle, same meter"
      - "keep the direction, forget the size"
      - "Still up?"
    colors:
      candidates: data.observed
      score: data.params
      failureFlag: data.error
      background: bg
      labels: text
    estimatedSeconds: 52

---
session: dotprod
aha: "The dot product is a similarity meter."
spineVersion: 1
locked: 2026-07-04
scope: >-
  Plain dot product as an alignment score, in two dimensions, four
  candidates. No cosine, no matrices, no attention; those are breadcrumbs.
compromises:
  - id: toy-numbers
    phrase: "the numbers in this session are chosen for readability"
  - id: two-dimensions
    phrase: "real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions"
---

# Spine: dotprod

The session's constitution. Every downstream artifact answers to this file.

## 1. The aha (canonical sentence, string-matched everywhere)

**"The dot product is a similarity meter."**

Falsification test the session must pass on screen: the probe sweep shows
the score climbing with alignment, reading zero across, and going negative
against; the meter framing survives the misconception check (a big score is
agreement of direction, not identity of items).

## 2. The locked beat list (stable ids; one new idea each)

| id | Beat | One-line intent | Est. sec |
|----|------|-----------------|----------|
| beat-01 | Hook | The recommendation moment: two lists of numbers agreed; how do you score a match? | 24 |
| beat-02 | Felt elemental unit | Multiply pairwise and add; the learner sweeps the probe and feels the score move; misconception (similarity means identical) voiced and refuted | 34 |
| beat-03 | Aha | The behavior gets its name: agree positive, across zero, oppose negative; the sentence trues up in mint | 28 |
| beat-04 | Close | Retrieval prompt, the length problem as the walk-home gap, extensions menu | 26 |

Total 112s estimated; hard cap 540s (L10).

## 3. The metaphor registry (with honesty boundaries)

Primary, load-bearing beats 2 through 4: **the meter**. A physical dial
whose needle answers a comparison: agree, ignore, oppose. Exact as far as
sign and monotone-in-alignment go. Declared strains, disclosed in-session:

1. the numbers in this session are chosen for readability; real embedding
   coordinates are neither small nor round (beat-02, page callout).
2. real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions; two dimensions is the
   picture, not the territory. Nothing in the mechanism changes with
   dimension count (beat-03, page callout).

## 4. Color assignments (semantic cast, locked)

| Concept | Token |
|---|---|
| the probe (the thing asking) | data.heat (amber) |
| the candidates (the data interrogated) | data.observed (periwinkle) |
| the score bars / meter needle | data.params (lilac) |
| the aha true-up | data.fit (mint) |
| labels | text (cream) |
| background | bg (10pm Sky) |

## 5. Extensions sketch (Still up?)

- talk lane: have an LLM defend, then attack, the meter metaphor; a good
  conversation surfaces the length problem unprompted.
- read lane: the attention paper (arXiv 1706.03762) is where tonight's
  score reappears inside a softmax; Bahdanau (arXiv 1409.0473) is where
  learned alignment scores first shipped.
- build lane: a NumPy meter over toy "song" vectors with a ranking check.

input.content:
# How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?

## The score behind the song

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

No one at the company vouched for that band. No genre tag did the work. Two columns of numbers slid past each other, and a single readout counted up until it said *match*.

So here is the question of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?

Hold that question. One answer to it runs half of modern machine learning, and it fits on the back of a napkin.

## What does a taste even look like as numbers?

Strip it down. A taste is an arrow. Each direction is a quality, and the length is how hard the song leans that way. Loud, mellow, fast, sad: pick your axes, and every song becomes an arrow pointing somewhere in that space.

Now the recipe for a score. Take one probe arrow (that's you, or the song you just loved) and a few candidate arrows (the songs you might get next). Multiply them entry by entry. Add up the products. That single sum is the score.

That's the whole machine. Multiply the matched entries, add them, read the number.

Before you run it, call your shot. Take one probe and four candidates. Which candidate ends up on top, and does any score go negative?

Most people guess that the closest-looking candidate wins and nothing dips below zero, because how could similarity ever be less than nothing? Keep that guess. We're about to spend it.

Now turn the probe and watch the scores answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the *same*, right?

Watch again. The scores moved while the candidates never moved at all. Only the probe turned. So the number can't be reading sameness of the items, because the items sat perfectly still the whole time. It's reading agreement of direction.

> Honest edges: the numbers in this session are chosen for readability. Real embedding coordinates are neither small nor round; nobody's taste vector is a tidy `3, 2, 0, -2`. The tidiness is a teaching prop, not a claim about the world.

The obstruction here isn't you being sloppy. It's that "similar" in plain English smuggles in "the same," and the meter refuses that smuggling. Agreement is a weaker, more honest thing than identity.

## What is this machine actually measuring?

Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe *along* a candidate and the sum climbs. Point it *across* and the sum dies to zero. Point it *against* and the sum goes negative.

One multiply and add, and it behaves like a meter. A dial with three regions marked: agree, ignore, oppose. Positive when the arrows point the same way. Zero when they're at right angles and have nothing to say about each other. Negative when they pull apart.

So say it plainly: the dot product is a similarity meter.

Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. And the reading tracks direction, because direction is all the sweep ever changed. From here on we can write that sum with its real name, $a \cdot b$, and mean exactly this dial.

> Honest edges: real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions. Two dimensions is the picture on the page, not the territory. Nothing in the machine changes when you add axes: you still multiply matched entries and add, whether there are two of them or two thousand. The dial just gets harder to draw.

A meter you can turn by hand is worth more than a formula you can only recite.

## Where does the same meter show up, and where does it break?

Time to close the loop before you read on. Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

While you think, one detour worth naming. Swap the songs for a job posting. Line up what you bring against what the role wants: Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit.

Different aisle, same meter. The multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers *meant*. It doesn't know songs from resumes. And that indifference is exactly why it travels everywhere.

Now the honest look, because a good meter is one you know how to fool. Stretch a candidate longer without turning it at all, and its score climbs anyway. Same direction, bigger number, higher rank. The length snuck in through the back.

In practice that means the loudest, longest song wins on sheer size, and the padded resume wins on sheer bulk. The meter can't tell an honest match from a big one. It reads direction *and* magnitude, and right now we only wanted direction.

That gap is the whole reason the next session exists.

For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

Still up? The menu below has three ways to keep going.

# How does a data center decide two songs share your taste?

## The score behind the song

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. No human picked it for you. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

That is the whole magic trick, and it is smaller than it looks. One list stood for you. One list stood for the song. Something turned the pair into a single number, and that number was high enough to hit play.

So here is the question of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?

Hold that question before we reach for any definition. One answer to it runs half of modern machine learning. We are going to build that answer with our hands, not memorize it.

## Turn the probe and feel it

Strip it down until it fits in your palm. A taste is an arrow. Each direction is a quality, some axis like "loud" or "mellow," and the length along that direction is how hard the song leans that way. Two dimensions, so we can actually see the thing.

Now picture one amber probe arrow, that is you, and four candidate arrows, four songs sitting still. Here is the rule for scoring a match: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. That single sum is the score. One multiply, one add.

> Honest edges: the numbers in this session are chosen for readability. Real embedding coordinates are neither small nor round. They are long ugly decimals a machine never has to look at. We use tidy integers so your eyes can do the arithmetic the GPU does silently.

Before you read on, call it. Which candidate ends up with the highest score, and does any score go negative? Say an answer out loud. A prediction you can be wrong about is worth ten definitions you nod along to.

The tempting guess is that the candidate that looks most like the probe wins, and that no score dips below zero, because how could similarity be less than nothing?

Now turn the probe and watch the scores answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the same, right?

Watch again, more carefully. The scores moved while the candidates never moved at all. Nothing about the songs changed. Only your direction changed. So the score cannot be reading sameness of items. It is reading agreement of direction.

And yes, scores go negative. Point against a candidate and the sum drops below zero, because some of those pairwise products come out negative and drag the sum down. Similarity, it turns out, has a basement.

The number measures which way they point, not what the things are.

## Name the machine you just used

Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs to its biggest. Point it across, at a right angle, and the sum dies to exactly zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative.

Three positions, three readings: agree, ignore, oppose. One multiply and one add, and it behaves like a meter with a needle that swings across those three words on a dial.

So say it plainly:

**The dot product is a similarity meter.**

Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. That is the machine. It has a name and a symbol now: we write it $a \cdot b$, the dot sitting between the two arrows to mean multiply-pairwise-and-add.

> Honest edges: real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions. Two dimensions is the picture, not the territory. Nothing in the mechanism changes when you add more axes. You still multiply entry by entry and add, there are just more entries. The dial in your head stays exactly as true at three hundred dimensions as it is at two.

And notice why the reading tracks direction and not size or identity. Direction is the only thing the sweep ever changed. The meter answers the question you actually moved.

## What the meter gets wrong

Time to close the loop. Do this from memory, not from scrolling up.

Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

If "multiply pairwise, then add" came back to you without a peek, you own $a \cdot b$ now. It is yours.

While that settles, one more honest look, because a meter you trust blindly is a meter that will lie to you. Take one candidate and stretch it longer without turning it at all. Its direction holds dead still. Its score climbs anyway.

That is a problem. It means a long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement. The meter cannot tell "points the same way" apart from "just bigger." A shout beats a whisper even when the whisper agrees with you more.

> Honest edges: the raw dot product rewards length as well as direction. We are shipping that flaw on purpose. We didn't hide it. It's the exact gap the next session opens on.

For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

Still up? The menu below has three ways to keep going: argue the metaphor with an LLM until it cracks, chase this same score into the attention papers where it reappears, or build the meter yourself in a few lines of NumPy.

# How does your app know two songs share your taste?

## The score behind the song

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. No human picked it for you. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

That's the whole magic trick, and it's smaller than it looks. One list stood for you. One list stood for the song. Something turned the pair into a single number, and that number was high enough to hit play.

So here is the question of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?

Hold that question before we reach for any definition. One answer to it runs half of modern machine learning. We're going to build that answer with our hands, not memorize it.

## What does a taste look like as numbers?

Strip it down until it fits in your palm. A taste is an arrow. Each direction is a quality, some axis like "loud" or "mellow," and the length along that direction is how hard the song leans that way. Two dimensions, so we can actually see the thing.

Now picture one amber probe arrow, that's you, and four candidate arrows, four songs sitting still. Here's the rule for scoring a match: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. That single sum is the score. One multiply, one add.

> Honest edges: the numbers in this session are chosen for readability. Real embedding coordinates are neither small nor round. We use tidy integers so your eyes can do the arithmetic the machine does silently.

Before you read on, call it. Which candidate ends up with the highest score, and does any score go negative? Say an answer out loud. A prediction you can be wrong about is worth ten definitions you nod along to.

The tempting guess is that the candidate that looks most like the probe wins, and that no score dips below zero, because how could similarity be less than nothing?

Now turn the probe and watch the scores answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the *same*, right?

Watch again, more carefully. The scores moved while the candidates never moved at all. Nothing about the songs changed. Only your direction changed. So the score can't be reading sameness of items. It's reading agreement of direction.

And yes, scores go negative. Point against a candidate and the sum drops below zero, because some of those pairwise products come out negative and drag the sum down. Similarity, it turns out, has a basement.

The number measures which way two arrows point, not what the things are.

## What is the machine you just used?

Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs to its biggest. Point it across, at a right angle, and the sum dies to exactly zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative.

Three positions, three readings: agree, ignore, oppose. One multiply and one add, and it behaves like a meter with a needle that swings across those three words on a dial.

So say it plainly:

**The dot product is a similarity meter.**

Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. That's the machine, and it has a symbol now: we write it $a \cdot b$, the dot between the two arrows standing for multiply-pairwise-and-add.

> Honest edges: real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions. Two dimensions is the picture, not the territory. Nothing in the mechanism changes when you add axes: you still multiply entry by entry and add, there are just more entries. The dial in your head stays exactly as true with hundreds of axes as it is with two.

And notice why the reading tracks direction and not size or identity. Direction is the only thing the sweep ever changed. The meter answers the question you actually moved.

## Where does the meter travel, and where does it break?

Time to close the loop. Do this from memory, not from scrolling up.

Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

If "multiply pairwise, then add" came back without a peek, you own $a \cdot b$ now. It's yours.

While that settles, one detour worth naming. Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Different aisle, same meter. The multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant, and that's exactly why it travels.

Now one more honest look, because a meter you trust blindly is a meter that will lie to you. Take one candidate and stretch it longer without turning it at all. Its direction holds dead still. Its score climbs anyway.

That's a problem. It means a long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement, and a padded resume can win on sheer length. The meter can't tell "points the same way" apart from "just bigger." A shout beats a whisper even when the whisper agrees with you more. That gap is exactly where the next session opens.

For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

Still up? The menu below has three ways to keep going: argue the metaphor with an LLM until it cracks, chase this same score into the attention papers where it reappears, or build the meter yourself in a few lines of NumPy.

# Script: dotprod

Final video narration, one section per storyboard beat. Narration
paragraphs are the spoken words, read at roughly 150 words per minute, with
no em-dashes and no emojis. Bracketed `[visual:]` paragraphs are the
video's stage directions: what is on screen while the words are spoken.

## beat-01: The score behind the song

[visual: connect-to-reality] Two columns of numbers slide in side by side, one labeled with the listener's taste and one with the song; paired entries light up in periwinkle and a single lilac score readout counts up as the comparison lands.

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

[visual: connect-to-reality] The question card holds over the frozen score, half the frame deliberately empty.

So here is the question of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers? Hold that question. One answer to it runs half of modern machine learning.

## beat-02: Turn the probe and feel it

[visual: dynamic-concept] One amber probe arrow and four periwinkle candidate arrows appear; under each candidate a lilac score bar with a numeric readout sits at its current value.

Strip it down. A taste is an arrow: each direction is a quality, and the length is how hard the song leans that way. Take one probe arrow and four candidate arrows. Multiply them entry by entry and add. That single sum is the score.

[visual: covary] A freeze frame carries the prediction card before anything moves.

Before you drag anything, call it: which candidate ends up on top, and does any score go negative?

[visual: covary] The probe sweeps through directions; each score bar climbs and dies as the probe turns toward and away from its candidate; after the sweep the probe parks and the four scores land as integers on the bars.

Now turn the probe and watch the bars answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the same, right? Watch again. The bars moved while the candidates never moved at all. The score reads agreement of direction, not sameness.

## beat-03: Name the machine you just used

[visual: connect] The three probe positions replay as stills: along, across, against, each with its frozen score. The stills collapse into one lilac meter dial whose needle swings from oppose through ignore to agree as the sweep replays.

Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply and add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.

[visual: symbol-sense] The aha sentence trues up on screen, freehand to exact with a mint glow. A small periwinkle mono annotation, a . b, sits on the score readout.

So say it plainly: the dot product is a similarity meter. Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. And the reading tracks direction, because direction is all the sweep ever changed.

## beat-04: What the meter gets wrong

[visual: dynamic-process] The retrieval prompt holds as a card while the meter idles.

Time to close the loop. Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

[visual: connect-to-reality] The song columns swap for two new ones labeled "you" and "the role": Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Matched entries multiply and the products pool into the same lilac readout on the same dial.

While you think, one detour worth naming. Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Different aisle, same meter. The multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant, and that is exactly why it travels.

[visual: dynamic-process] One periwinkle candidate stretches longer; its lilac score climbs while its direction holds still; a small rose flag marks the unearned win.

Now the honest look. Stretch a candidate longer and its score climbs, even though its direction never moved. Long songs would win on sheer loudness, and padded resumes would win on sheer length.

[visual: connect-to-reality] The walk-home question lands as the closing card, and a quiet chip points to the extensions menu on the page.

For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size? Still up? The menu below has three ways to keep going.

aha: "The dot product is a similarity meter."

  Plain dot product as an alignment score, in two dimensions, four
  candidates. No cosine, no matrices, no attention; those are breadcrumbs.

    phrase: "real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions"
---

# Spine: dotprod

The session's constitution. Every downstream artifact answers to this file.

## 1. The aha (canonical sentence, string-matched everywhere)

**"The dot product is a similarity meter."**

Falsification test the session must pass on screen: the probe sweep shows
the score climbing with alignment, reading zero across, and going negative
against; the meter framing survives the misconception check (a big score is
agreement of direction, not identity of items).

## 2. The locked beat list (stable ids; one new idea each)

| id | Beat | One-line intent | Est. sec |
|----|------|-----------------|----------|
| beat-01 | Hook | The recommendation moment: two lists of numbers agreed; how do you score a match? | 24 |
| beat-02 | Felt elemental unit | Multiply pairwise and add; the learner sweeps the probe and feels the score move; misconception (similarity means identical) voiced and refuted | 34 |
| beat-03 | Aha | The behavior gets its name: agree positive, across zero, oppose negative; the sentence trues up in mint | 28 |
| beat-04 | Close | Retrieval prompt, the length problem as the walk-home gap, extensions menu | 26 |

Total 112s estimated; hard cap 540s (L10).

## 3. The metaphor registry (with honesty boundaries)

Primary, load-bearing beats 2 through 4: **the meter**. A physical dial
whose needle answers a comparison: agree, ignore, oppose. Exact as far as
sign and monotone-in-alignment go. Declared strains, disclosed in-session:

1. the numbers in this session are chosen for readability; real embedding
   coordinates are neither small nor round (beat-02, page callout).
2. real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions; two dimensions is the
   picture, not the territory. Nothing in the mechanism changes with
   dimension count (beat-03, page callout).

## 4. Color assignments (semantic cast, locked)

| Concept | Token |
|---|---|
| the probe (the thing asking) | data.heat (amber) |
| the candidates (the data interrogated) | data.observed (periwinkle) |
| the score bars / meter needle | data.params (lilac) |
| the aha true-up | data.fit (mint) |
| labels | text (cream) |
| background | bg (10pm Sky) |

## 5. Extensions sketch (Still up?)

- talk lane: have an LLM defend, then attack, the meter metaphor; a good
  conversation surfaces the length problem unprompted.
- read lane: the attention paper (arXiv 1706.03762) is where tonight's
  score reappears inside a softmax; Bahdanau (arXiv 1409.0473) is where
  learned alignment scores first shipped.
- build lane: a NumPy meter over toy "song" vectors with a ranking check.

input.content:
# How does your app know a stranger's song is your taste?

## The score behind the song

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. No human on staff listened to it and thought of you. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

That is the whole trick, and it is stranger than it sounds. Your taste is a list of numbers. The song is a list of numbers. And some tiny piece of arithmetic looked at both and produced a verdict.

So here is the question of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?

Hold that question before we define anything. One answer to it runs half of modern machine learning, which is a lot of weight for one sum to carry.

Before you read on, guess the shape of the answer. If someone handed you two lists of numbers and said "tell me how well they match," what is the first operation you would reach for?

## Turn the probe and feel it

Let's strip it down until we can hold it in one hand.

A taste is an arrow. Each direction stands for a quality, and the length says how hard the song leans that way. Loud, mellow, fast, sad: pick two of those as your axes and every song becomes an arrow pointing somewhere on the plane.

Now take one probe arrow, the thing doing the asking, and four candidate arrows, the songs you might get served. Here is the recipe for the score. Multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up those products. That single sum is the match score.

> Honest edges: the numbers in this session are chosen for readability. Real embedding coordinates are neither small nor round; nobody's taste vector is a tidy $3, 2, 0, -2$. We picked friendly integers so the meter's behavior, not the bookkeeping, is what you remember.

Before you drag anything, call it. Which candidate ends up on top, and does any score go negative?

Most people guess the arrow that looks most like the probe wins, and that nothing goes below zero, because how could a similarity be less than nothing? Watch what actually happens.

Turn the probe and the bars answer. Point it at a candidate and that candidate's score climbs. Turn away and the score dies. Keep turning, past ninety degrees, and the score drops clean through zero into the negatives.

So a big score means two songs are the same, right? Watch again, and watch closely: the bars moved while the candidates never budged. Nothing about the songs changed. Only the direction of your probe changed. The score was never reading sameness. It was reading agreement of direction.

Agreement is not identity. That distinction is the hinge the rest of the night swings on.

Say it back to yourself before moving on: in your own words, what did the score respond to, and what did it ignore?

## Name the machine you just used

Look at what your hands just learned, because it already has a name.

Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs to its biggest value. Point it across, at a right angle, and the sum dies to exactly zero. Point it against, straight opposite, and the sum goes negative. One multiply and add, and it behaves like a dial with three labeled zones: agree, ignore, oppose.

So say it plainly: The dot product is a similarity meter.

Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. And that reading tracks direction, because direction is the only thing the sweep ever changed. The machine you built by turning an arrow is the same $a \cdot b$ that shows up in the papers.

> Honest edges: real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions. Two dimensions is the picture, not the territory; we drew a plane because planes fit on a page. Nothing in the mechanism changes when the arrows grow more coordinates. You still multiply matched entries and add. The needle on the dial does not care whether it summed two products or five hundred.

Here is the meter in one breath: $a \cdot b$ is positive when the arrows agree, zero when they stand crossways, negative when they fight.

Now make it yours. Without looking up, describe the meter to an imaginary friend: what does the needle do as one arrow turns from pointing with the other to pointing against it?

## What the meter gets wrong

Time to close the loop. Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

While you work that out, one detour worth naming. Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Multiply the matched entries, add them up, and you get one number for the fit. Different aisle, same meter. The multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant, and that is exactly why it travels.

Now the honest look, because the meter has a real flaw and we are not going to hide it. Stretch a candidate arrow longer without turning it at all. Its direction holds perfectly still, and yet its score climbs anyway. The alignment did not improve. The arrow just got bigger.

Play that out and it stings. A long, loud song would win on sheer volume. A padded resume would win on sheer length. The meter, left raw, mistakes "more" for "better matched," which is the kind of bug that hires the wrong person.

That gap is the whole opening for what comes next.

For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

Still up? The extensions menu below has three ways to keep going.

# How does your app know two songs share your taste?

## The score behind the song

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. No human picked it for you. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

That's the whole magic trick, and it's smaller than it looks. One list stood for you. One list stood for the song. Something turned the pair into a single number, and that number was high enough to hit play.

So here is the question of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?

Hold that question before we reach for any definition. One answer to it runs half of modern machine learning. We're going to build that answer with our hands, not memorize it.

## What does a taste look like as numbers?

Strip it down until it fits in your palm. A taste is an arrow. Each direction is a quality, some axis like "loud" or "mellow," and the length along that direction is how hard the song leans that way. Two dimensions, so we can actually see the thing.

Now picture one amber probe arrow, that's you, and four candidate arrows, four songs sitting still. Here's the rule for scoring a match: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. That single sum is the score. One multiply, one add.

> Honest edges: the numbers in this session are chosen for readability. Real embedding coordinates are neither small nor round. We use tidy integers so your eyes can do the arithmetic the machine does silently.

Before you read on, call it. Which candidate ends up with the highest score, and does any score go negative? Say an answer out loud. A prediction you can be wrong about is worth ten definitions you nod along to.

The tempting guess is that the candidate that looks most like the probe wins, and that no score dips below zero, because how could similarity be less than nothing?

Now turn the probe and watch the scores answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the *same*, right?

Watch again, more carefully. The scores moved while the candidates never moved at all. Nothing about the songs changed. Only your direction changed. So the score can't be reading sameness of items. It's reading agreement of direction.

And yes, scores go negative. Point against a candidate and the sum drops below zero, because some of those pairwise products come out negative and drag the sum down. Similarity, it turns out, has a basement. Before you scroll on, call one more reading: what would a score of exactly zero mean for two songs? Not opposed, not aligned. Say it in your own words, then check yourself against the sweep.

The number measures which way two arrows point, not what the things are.

## What is the machine you just used?

Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs to its biggest. Point it across, at a right angle, and the sum dies to exactly zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative.

Three positions, three readings: agree, ignore, oppose. One multiply and one add, and it behaves like a meter with a needle that swings across those three words on a dial.

So say it plainly:

**The dot product is a similarity meter.**

Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. That's the machine, and it has a symbol now: we write it $a \cdot b$, the dot between the two arrows standing for multiply-pairwise-and-add.

> Honest edges: real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions. Two dimensions is the picture, not the territory. Nothing in the mechanism changes when you add axes: you still multiply entry by entry and add, there are just more entries. The dial in your head stays exactly as true with hundreds of axes as it is with two.

And notice why the reading tracks direction and not size or identity. Direction is the only thing the sweep ever changed. The meter answers the question you actually moved. Close the loop yourself before the next section: the meter has three readings worth knowing, positive, zero, and negative. State what each one says about two vectors, out loud or on paper, in your own words.

## Where does the meter travel, and where does it break?

Time to close the loop. Do this from memory, not from scrolling up.

Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

If "multiply pairwise, then add" came back without a peek, you own $a \cdot b$ now. It's yours.

While that settles, one detour worth naming. Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Different aisle, same meter. The multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant, and that's exactly why it travels.

Now one more honest look, because a meter you trust blindly is a meter that will lie to you. Take one candidate and stretch it longer without turning it at all. Its direction holds dead still. Its score climbs anyway.

That's a problem. It means a long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement, and a padded resume can win on sheer length. The meter can't tell "points the same way" apart from "just bigger." A shout beats a whisper even when the whisper agrees with you more. That gap is exactly where the next session opens.

For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

Still up? The menu below has three ways to keep going: argue the metaphor with an LLM until it cracks, chase this same score into the attention papers where it reappears, or build the meter yourself in a few lines of NumPy.

39

file.content:
# Script: dotprod

Final video narration, one section per storyboard beat. Narration
paragraphs are the spoken words, read at roughly 150 words per minute, with
no em-dashes and no emojis. Bracketed `[visual:]` paragraphs are the
video's stage directions: what is on screen while the words are spoken.

## beat-01: The score behind the song

[visual: connect-to-reality] Two columns of numbers slide in side by side, one labeled with the listener's taste and one with the song; paired entries light up in periwinkle and a single lilac score readout counts up as the comparison lands.

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

[visual: connect-to-reality] The question card holds over the frozen score, half the frame deliberately empty.

So here is the question of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers? Hold that question. One answer to it runs half of modern machine learning.

## beat-02: Turn the probe and feel it

[visual: dynamic-concept] One amber probe arrow and four periwinkle candidate arrows appear; under each candidate a lilac score bar with a numeric readout sits at its current value.

Strip it down. A taste is an arrow: each direction is a quality, and the length is how hard the song leans that way. Take one probe arrow and four candidate arrows. Multiply them entry by entry and add. That single sum is the score.

[visual: covary] A freeze frame carries the prediction card before anything moves.

Before you drag anything, call it: which candidate ends up on top, and does any score go negative?

[visual: covary] The probe sweeps through directions; each score bar climbs and dies as the probe turns toward and away from its candidate; after the sweep the probe parks and the four scores land as integers on the bars.

Now turn the probe and watch the bars answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the same, right? Watch again. The bars moved while the candidates never moved at all. The score reads agreement of direction, not sameness.

## beat-03: Name the machine you just used

[visual: connect] The three probe positions replay as stills: along, across, against, each with its frozen score. The stills collapse into one lilac meter dial whose needle swings from oppose through ignore to agree as the sweep replays.

Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply and add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.

[visual: symbol-sense] The aha sentence trues up on screen, freehand to exact with a mint glow. A small periwinkle mono annotation, a . b, sits on the score readout.

So say it plainly: the dot product is a similarity meter. Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. And the reading tracks direction, because direction is all the sweep ever changed.

## beat-04: What the meter gets wrong

[visual: dynamic-process] The retrieval prompt holds as a card while the meter idles.

Time to close the loop. Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

[visual: connect-to-reality] The song columns swap for two new ones labeled "you" and "the role": Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Matched entries multiply and the products pool into the same lilac readout on the same dial.

While you think, one detour worth naming. Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Different aisle, same meter. The multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant, and that is exactly why it travels.

[visual: dynamic-process] A guess card holds: one candidate outlined, an arrow hinting it is about to grow, the score readout hidden.

Now the honest look, and guess before you watch. If a candidate's numbers just get bigger, same direction, longer arrow, does its score change?

[visual: dynamic-process] One periwinkle candidate stretches longer; its lilac score climbs while its direction holds still; a small rose flag marks the unearned win.

It climbs. The direction never moved, and the score climbed anyway. Long songs would win on sheer loudness, and padded resumes would win on sheer length.

[visual: connect-to-reality] The walk-home question lands as the closing card, and a quiet chip points to the extensions menu on the page.

For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size? Still up? The menu below has three ways to keep going.
